metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default JWKS HTTP fetch timeout value explicitly in the plan (e.g. `auth_jwt_jwks_connect_timeout` / `auth_jwt_jwks_read_timeout` = 10 s, or a single `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` default), so the AC oracle 'a timeout is passed to the client' is verifiable against a specific expected value.
evidence: 'with a bounded HTTP timeout' (Security section) — no numeric value given
AC 7 oracle says only 'a timeout is passed to the client', not what value constitutes 'bounded'
All other numeric parameters (leeway=60, cooldown=30) have explicit defaults stated
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'bounded HTTP timeout' for JWKS fetches but never states the actual default timeout value. An executor must independently choose this number.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded HTTP timeout'; AC 7 observable oracle — 'a timeout is passed to the client'
scenarios: Executor chooses a 30-second timeout; a different executor chooses 5 seconds; the two implementations behave differently under slow JWKS endpoints.
A code reviewer cannot verify correctness without an agreed default, making the AC oracle ('a timeout is passed') vacuously satisfiable with any non-zero value.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Security section and the config-key list: `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default `10`) — passed as the `timeout` argument to `PyJWKClient`. Update AC 7's observable oracle to: 'the client is constructed with `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (default 10 s)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the unknown-kid refetch cooldown uses an `asyncio.Lock` (or equivalent serialisation primitive) to make the check-and-update of `last_refetch_time` atomic, ensuring the 'at most one fetch per cooldown window' guarantee holds under concurrent async requests.
evidence: 'a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time' — no lock or atomic operation described
'at most one refetch per cooldown window' — this invariant cannot hold without serialisation when multiple async tasks execute concurrently
FastMCP runs on an asyncio event loop; concurrent requests share the same process and the same verifier instance

The AC test only checks the count against a stateful fake but does not specify that the implementation uses a lock — so an implementer may produce a racy implementation that still passes the serial unit test
findings.finding: The unknown-kid refetch cooldown mechanism is a check-then-act TOCTOU race in the async context. The plan describes 'a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time,' but in FastMCP's async event loop multiple concurrent requests that each see an unknown kid will each read the last-refetch timestamp, each find the cooldown elapsed, and each fire a JWKS fetch simultaneously — violating the plan's own guarantee of 'at most one JWKS fetch per cooldown window.' An asyncio.Lock (or equivalent serialisation) around the check-and-update of the last-refetch timestamp is required but is not mentioned.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS' bullet; AC line 'Unit test asserts unknown-kid cooldown'
scenarios: Two requests with distinct unknown kids arrive within microseconds; both read last_refetch=T0, both compute T_now - T0 > cooldown=True, both dispatch a JWKS fetch — the invariant is broken
Under load (many concurrent connections), an attacker with random kids could trigger O(concurrency) fetches per cooldown window rather than O(1)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security / 'Key rotation without DoS' bullet: 'The cooldown check-and-update is guarded by an `asyncio.Lock` so that concurrent async requests see the refetch as already in-progress once the first caller acquires the lock, preventing a burst from firing more than one fetch.'

findings.finding: The JWKS HTTP fetch timeout is called 'bounded' in Security and tested in AC 7, but the plan never states the default timeout value or whether it is a named config key — an executor must independently decide both.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded HTTP timeout'); AC 7 ('a timeout is passed to the client')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default 10) to the registered config keys list and update the Security bullet to read 'bounded HTTP timeout (default 10 s, controlled by `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout`)'; update AC 7's oracle to '…a timeout of `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` seconds is passed to the client'.

findings.finding: The 'thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time' for the unknown-kid cooldown is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) pattern that breaks under async concurrency: two coroutines can simultaneously read the timestamp, both find the cooldown elapsed, and both trigger a JWKS fetch within the same window — violating the plan's hard 'at most one refetch per cooldown window' guarantee.
findings.location: Approach & rationale ('implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself (a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time)') / Security ('at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds') / AC 'a burst of requests with distinct unknown kids within one cooldown window triggers at most one JWKS fetch'
findings.suggested_fix: Use an `asyncio.Lock` to guard the check-then-act: acquire the lock, re-check the cooldown (double-checked locking), perform the fetch if still needed, update the timestamp, release. Alternatively, track an `asyncio.Event` for 'fetch in progress' and have subsequent waiters await the event rather than triggering their own fetch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the cooldown check-then-update is guarded by an `asyncio.Lock` (or threading lock if synchronous) so that concurrent requests cannot simultaneously pass the cooldown check and trigger multiple JWKS fetches within the same window.
evidence: 'a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time' — no mention of locking or atomicity
The AC asserts 'at most one JWKS fetch' for a burst, which is violated by the race
In an async context, two coroutines can interleave between the read of `last_refetch` and the write of the new timestamp
findings.finding: The unknown-kid refetch cooldown is described as 'a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time', but under concurrent async (or threaded) request handling, multiple coroutines can simultaneously read `last_refetch`, both determine the cooldown has elapsed, and both trigger a JWKS fetch — a classic check-then-act TOCTOU race. The plan does not specify a lock, `asyncio.Lock`, or atomic compare-and-swap to guard this state mutation.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale — 'a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time'; Security — 'caps refetch on an unknown kid to at most one refetch per cooldown window'; AC — 'a burst of requests with distinct unknown kids … triggers at most one JWKS fetch'
scenarios: Burst of 50 concurrent requests with unknown kids all arrive simultaneously; all 50 pass the cooldown check before any one of them updates the timestamp, triggering 50 JWKS fetches instead of 1
Under asyncio, the race window is between an `await` and the next statement — realistic without an explicit lock
findings.suggested_fix: Use an `asyncio.Lock`: acquire it before checking `last_refetch`, update the timestamp and perform the fetch within the lock, then release. This serializes refetch decisions and guarantees at-most-one fetch per cooldown window.

findings.finding: The plan declares a 'bounded HTTP timeout' for the JWKS fetch as a hard security requirement, but never specifies the default timeout value or its upper bound. The AC observable oracle is only 'a timeout is passed to the client', which passes even if the implementation chooses 300 seconds — effectively unbounded under load, enabling a slow-JWKS-server DoS that holds MCP request goroutines open.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet; AC7 — 'a timeout is passed to the client'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The JWKS HTTP fetch timeout MUST default to ≤5 seconds (configurable via `auth_jwt_jwks_fetch_timeout`, no ceiling higher than 30 s).' Update AC7 to: 'a timeout ≤5 s is passed to the client (observable oracle: the value passed to PyJWKClient/urllib is ≤5 s).'

findings.finding: The cooldown tracker (last refetch timestamp) is shared mutable state that multiple concurrent async tasks may read-check-then-write simultaneously. The plan asserts 'at most one refetch per cooldown window' as a security property but does not specify atomic mutation (lock, CAS, or asyncio.Lock), leaving a TOCTOU race: two concurrent requests with distinct unknown kids can both observe that the cooldown has elapsed, both call into PyJWKClient, and both trigger a JWKS fetch — violating the stated security invariant.
findings.location: Security ('key rotation without DoS') / AC 'Unit test asserts unknown-kid cooldown: a burst of requests…triggers at most one JWKS fetch'
findings.suggested_fix: Guard the cooldown block with an `asyncio.Lock`: `async with self._refetch_lock: if now - self._last_refetch > cooldown: self._last_refetch = now; <do fetch>`. This makes the check-then-act atomic within the async context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that synchronous PyJWKClient HTTP calls are offloaded via `asyncio.to_thread` (consistent with the codebase's established pattern for blocking I/O in async contexts) so JWKS fetches do not block the FastMCP event loop.
evidence: PyJWKClient uses `urllib` / `requests`-style synchronous HTTP internally
The plan states 'a bounded HTTP timeout' but does not say the call is wrapped in asyncio.to_thread or any other async bridge
The codebase pattern for blocking I/O in async contexts is asyncio.to_thread (confirmed: voter.py, opcert_service/app.py, gerrit_client.py all use this idiom)
Absence of any async bridging instruction in the plan
findings.finding: PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt() (and the underlying JWKS HTTP fetch) is synchronous and blocking. The plan places this verifier in an async FastMCP server context but says nothing about offloading the blocking call to a thread pool (e.g. asyncio.to_thread). A cache miss or key rotation event will block the entire event loop for the duration of the HTTP round-trip, serialising all concurrent MCP requests behind the JWKS fetch and creating a latency spike observable as a denial-of-service amplifier.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient'; Security — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet; AC 'Unit test asserts a JWKS fetch failure raises'
scenarios: A JWKS cache miss under load: the event loop is blocked for the full HTTP timeout (potentially several seconds), starving all other async MCP handlers
Key rotation event: every in-flight request that encounters the new kid blocks the loop while one fetch serialises them all
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Because PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt() is synchronous, all JWKS fetches (cache-miss and cooldown-gated refetch) are dispatched via `asyncio.to_thread(...)` to avoid blocking the FastMCP event loop — consistent with the project's established pattern (voter.py, opcert_service).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the error-response sanitization contract in the Security section: specify that PyJWT exception details are logged at DEBUG/WARNING internally and that only a generic rejection signal (None or a sanitized exception type with no claim-level detail) propagates toward the caller/composite layer.
evidence: Plan says 'a definitively invalid token returns None' and 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises' — no sanitization contract stated
PyJWT exceptions (DecodeError, InvalidClaimError, ExpiredSignatureError, etc.) carry human-readable messages that may expose expected claim values or key IDs
Absence of any 'exception messages are logged internally and not surfaced to callers' requirement in the Security section or ACs
findings.finding: The plan does not declare the error-response posture for the inbound boundary-crossing path. PyJWT raises structured exceptions whose `.args` / `.message` fields may include claim names, expected vs. actual issuer/audience values, or key IDs. The plan specifies 'returns None' for an invalid token and 'raises' for a JWKS failure, but does not state that exception detail is sanitized before propagation, leaving open an information-disclosure channel toward lower-trust callers.
findings.location: Security section — last bullet ('A JWKS-fetch failure raises … a definitively invalid token returns None'); AC line 'Unit test asserts a JWKS fetch failure raises'
scenarios: An attacker probing the endpoint receives detailed error messages (e.g., 'Invalid audience: expected https://api.example.com, got https://evil.com') that reveal the expected audience value and token structure, aiding targeted forgery attempts.
A JWKS fetch failure exception propagates its stack trace or URL through the MCP transport layer to the client, disclosing internal infrastructure details.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to the Security section: 'Error sanitization: PyJWT exceptions are caught internally; only a generic non-acceptance signal (None / a sanitized AuthError with no claim-level detail) is propagated to the composite layer. Raw exception messages are logged at DEBUG/WARNING level internally and never included in HTTP responses.' Add a corresponding AC asserting that a rejected token's failure path does not expose the expected issuer/audience values in any raised exception propagated beyond the verifier boundary.

findings.finding: The 'symmetric algorithm' category used in the startup-refusal rule is referenced as a security-critical enum but its membership is never enumerated — the plan gives only `HS256` as an example, leaving `HS384`, `HS512`, and other HMAC/symmetric alg IDs as implementer-defined.
findings.location: Security section: 'refuse to start if any symmetric algorithm (e.g. `HS256`) is configured'
findings.suggested_fix: In Security: 'Refuse to start if `auth_jwt_algorithms` contains any of: `HS256`, `HS384`, `HS512`, `none`, or any other non-asymmetric identifier not in {RS256, RS384, RS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, PS256, PS384, PS512}.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the synchronous PyJWKClient JWKS fetch is offloaded via `asyncio.to_thread` (or replaced with an async JWKS client) so the async event loop is never blocked during a network fetch.
evidence: 'Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + kid lookup)' — PyJWKClient uses synchronous `urllib`/`requests` under the hood
No mention of `asyncio.to_thread`, an async JWKS client, or thread-pool offload anywhere in the plan
FastMCP/MCP SDK is async-first; `_mcp_auth.py` operates in an async context
findings.finding: The JWKS HTTP fetch via PyJWKClient is synchronous/blocking. The plan does not specify that the fetch is offloaded to a thread pool (e.g., `asyncio.to_thread`) or replaced with an async HTTP client. In an async MCP server, a blocking network call on the token-verification hot path will stall the event loop for the entire duration of the fetch (typically 50–500 ms), blocking all concurrent requests regardless of scale.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale; Security — 'bounded HTTP timeout'; AC — 'a timeout is passed to the client'
scenarios: A legitimate key rotation triggers a refetch; the event loop stalls for the network round-trip, dropping or delaying all concurrent requests
During startup or cold cache, the first token verification stalls the event loop
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt` call in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` inside the async `verify` method, or adopt an async JWKS client (e.g., `python-jose` async path or a custom `httpx`-based fetcher) for the refetch path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/env-vars.md` regeneration to the plan's edit targets: running `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` (or noting it runs automatically as part of `make test`'s CI drift gate) must be included so the env-var registry drift gate doesn't fail when `_mcp_auth.py` introduces new `os.environ` reads.
evidence: scripts/gen_env_registry.py scans src/rebar via AST and generates docs/env-vars.md; CI drift gate runs `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py --check` in both gerrit-verify.yaml (line 219) and test.yml (line 260)
_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES in test_ci_workflow_parity.py includes 'env-var registry drift gate': 'scripts/gen_env_registry.py' — this gate runs on every PR
The plan mentions 'regenerated reference docs' but its AC only references MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json and scripts/check_server_manifest.py — no mention of gen_env_registry.py or docs/env-vars.md
ABSENCE: docs/env-vars.md and gen_env_registry.py are not named anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan names 'regenerates the reference docs' and 'registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json' but does NOT enumerate docs/env-vars.md (generated by scripts/gen_env_registry.py) as an artifact that needs regenerating. The gen_env_registry.py script performs an AST scan of src/rebar for os.environ reads and will pick up any new direct env-var reads in _mcp_auth.py; the CI env-var registry drift gate (present in both gerrit-verify.yaml and test.yml per test_ci_workflow_parity.py) will fail unless docs/env-vars.md is regenerated. This is an unenumerated consumer/artifact.
findings.location: AC line: 'registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json, appear in the regenerated reference docs'
scenarios: Developer implements _mcp_auth.py with direct os.environ.get('AUTH_JWT_JWKS_URI') reads; CI env-var registry drift gate fails because docs/env-vars.md is stale; make test fails even though all named ACs pass.
If auth_jwt_* keys are read via a helper not in KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS, the generator misses them silently — but standard reads will be caught.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's scope: 'Run `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` to regenerate `docs/env-vars.md` after adding env-var reads in `_mcp_auth.py`; the CI drift gate (`scripts/gen_env_registry.py --check`) requires this file to be current.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a distinct AC (or extend AC 3) that exercises a JWT carrying the `scp` claim and asserts the resulting `AccessToken.scopes` are populated correctly, providing explicit coverage for the `scope`/`scp` dual-claim mapping.
evidence: Plan body: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes, sets `expires_at` from `exp`'

AC 3: 'a JWT with correct `iss`/`aud`(=resource) → an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes AND `resource==auth_resource_server_url` and a positive `expires_at` (observable oracle: returned `AccessToken` fields)' — says nothing about exercising the `scp` variant
ABSENCE: no separate AC line tests a JWT with only the `scp` claim to confirm that code path is reached
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes `scope`/`scp`→scopes mapping as a deliverable, but no AC explicitly tests that a JWT carrying the `scp` claim (alternative spelling) is also mapped correctly to scopes — only 'expected scopes' is checked in the happy-path AC, without specifying that both claim-name variants are exercised.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes') vs AC line 3 ('an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes')
scenarios: An IdP that emits `scp` instead of `scope` (e.g. Microsoft Entra ID uses `scp`) would silently return empty scopes if only `scope` is implemented, but the test suite would not catch it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 3 to read: '…the expected scopes, tested with both a `scope`-claim token and a `scp`-claim token (observable oracle: `AccessToken.scopes` populated in both cases).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or a `## Security Architecture` section to `CLAUDE.md` (or equivalent persistent design doc) capturing the rationale for the custom kid-flood cooldown, SSRF guard, symmetric-algorithm startup rejection, and configurable `typ` check — so these non-obvious invariants survive beyond the ticket.
evidence: Plan is silent on any ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc update
'PyJWKClient's lifespan is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard, so this verifier implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself' — novel non-obvious decision with no persistent artifact
Security section lists 5+ non-obvious architectural choices (SSRF guard, symmetric-alg confusion, kid-flood, typ-check) — all boundary-crossing
T5e: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update'
findings.finding: The novel security architectural decisions (custom unknown-kid refetch cooldown, construction-time SSRF guard, symmetric-algorithm startup rejection) are documented only in the plan/ticket text and will not be discoverable by future maintainers from within the codebase itself — no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or inline design-doc update is planned.
findings.location: ## Approach & rationale / ## Security / ## Acceptance Criteria (no ADR/CLAUDE.md update mentioned)
scenarios: A future maintainer refactors PyJWKClient usage and unknowingly removes the custom cooldown, not knowing why it exists
A security auditor can't find the rationale for the SSRF guard or symmetric-alg rejection without reading the original ticket
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0003-jwks-verifier-security.md`) or a `### JWKS Verifier Security Invariants` section in `CLAUDE.md` summarizing: (1) why a custom cooldown wraps PyJWKClient rather than relying on `lifespan`; (2) why symmetric algorithms are rejected at construction; (3) why `jwks_uri` is HTTPS-only with private-host blocking; (4) the configurable `typ` check and when to enable it. Reference this from inline code comments on the verifier class.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 3) requiring a unit test that presents a JWT carrying an `scp` claim (not `scope`) and asserts the resulting `AccessToken.scopes` are correctly populated, giving the scp alias an end-state-observable oracle.
evidence: 'maps scope/scp→scopes, sets expires_at from exp' — both are stated features

AC 3 only asserts 'a JWT with correct iss/aud(=resource) → an AccessToken with the expected scopes' — does not specify which claim supplies the scopes, so an implementation that only reads 'scope' and ignores 'scp' would pass AC 3
ABSENCE: no AC requires a token carrying 'scp' (not 'scope') and asserts it maps correctly
findings.finding: The plan states 'maps scope/scp→scopes' as a non-deferred feature but no AC requires a test token that uses the 'scp' claim variant, leaving the scp→scopes mapping path without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: What section ('maps scope/scp→scopes'); AC 3
scenarios: A provider (e.g. Azure AD / Entra ID) that emits 'scp' instead of 'scope' in its access tokens would silently return zero scopes, failing at runtime despite passing all ACs
An implementer might only wire up 'scope' mapping and ship a broken 'scp' path undetected
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 3: 'Unit tests assert correct scope extraction from both the `scope` claim (space-delimited string) and the `scp` claim (array or space-delimited string), each producing the expected `AccessToken.scopes`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `mcp>=1.9` lists PyJWT as a declared (not accidental) dependency by inspecting its package metadata (e.g. `pip show mcp`), and update the rationale accordingly — or simply drop the 'already a transitive' claim and rely solely on the explicit `pyjwt[crypto]` addition.
evidence: Plan text: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`; `pyjwt[crypto]` makes the `cryptography` backend explicit for RS/ES verification'
pyproject.toml mcp extra only lists 'mcp>=1.9' with no explicit pyjwt pin
jwt.PyJWKClient resolves in the venv (consistent with transitive being present), but this alone doesn't confirm mcp is the source
findings.finding: The plan states 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' as a basis for the dependency story, but this transitive dependency claim is unverified — if `mcp>=1.9` does not pull PyJWT transitively, then the `pyjwt[crypto]` add to the `[mcp]` extra is still correct but the rationale is unsupported, and existing installs without `[mcp]` may be surprised.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`'
scenarios: A future `mcp` release drops PyJWT as a transitive (e.g. switches to joserfc or authlib) and the explicit `pyjwt[crypto]` in `[mcp]` becomes the only guarantee — the plan's rationale for *not* pinning it to core obscures this risk.
A reviewer or auditor questions the dependency justification and cannot confirm the chain.
findings.suggested_fix: Run `pip show mcp | grep -i requires` to check if pyjwt appears in mcp's declared dependencies. If it does not, replace 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' with 'we add `pyjwt[crypto]` explicitly to the `[mcp]` extra to guarantee the cryptography backend is available.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the SSRF guard validates the *resolved IP address* at fetch time (not only the hostname string at construction time) — e.g., by using a custom HTTP adapter/transport that extracts the connected socket address and applies the private-range check before the response is consumed, or by resolving the hostname at construction and pinning the IP for all subsequent fetches.
evidence: 'jwks_uri is fixed at construction from trusted config' — construction-time check on the URI string/hostname does not prevent runtime DNS resolution returning a private IP
PyJWKClient performs DNS resolution at each HTTP request (system resolver); no plan text specifies runtime IP validation of the resolved address
Plan explicitly claims SSRF protection as a hard requirement ('rejected by default') but the protection is incomplete without a post-resolution IP check
findings.finding: The SSRF guard performs a hostname/URI check at construction time, but PyJWKClient resolves DNS at each HTTP fetch. A DNS rebinding or DNS poisoning attack on the operator-configured IdP hostname can cause the runtime JWKS fetch to connect to a private/loopback address that the construction-time check already cleared, bypassing the private-host rejection entirely.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet; AC line 'a private/link-local host is rejected by default'
scenarios: Attacker poisons the DNS record for the operator-configured IdP hostname (e.g., via BGP hijack or DNS cache poisoning) so that subsequent JWKS fetches resolve to 169.254.169.254 (EC2 IMDS), allowing SSRF credential theft.

A legitimate IdP's hostname is later CNAMEd to an internal address (e.g., during IdP migration) and the verifier silently fetches from the internal host because the construction-time check passed at startup.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' security bullet, add: 'The resolved IP address of each JWKS fetch is also validated against the private-host block-list (not only the URI hostname at construction time), so DNS rebinding cannot bypass the guard.' In the AC, extend the SSRF test to assert that a jwks_uri whose hostname resolves to a private IP at fetch time (but not at construction time) is also rejected.

findings.finding: The `scp` scope-alias mapping is a named deliverable in the What section ("maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes") but no Acceptance Criterion explicitly requires testing the `scp` claim path. AC3 only says "expected scopes" without mandating a variant with the `scp` field, so a defective implementation that ignores `scp` would pass all ACs.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes') vs AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'Unit test asserts a JWT whose scopes are carried in `scp` (not `scope`) also yields an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes (observable oracle: returned `AccessToken.scopes` field).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Include a security note (e.g. a `> **Security:** For RFC 9068-compliant IdPs, set `auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT`…` admonition) directly in the S3-generated reference docs entry for `auth_jwt_expected_typ`, rather than deferring the recommendation entirely to S7.
evidence: 'S7 documents that operators SHOULD set auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT for RFC 9068-compliant IdPs' — deferral to a future story
AC2 regenerates reference docs for S3 but carries no requirement to include a security note for auth_jwt_expected_typ
Operators shipping S3 before S7 land will see the config key but not the security rationale
findings.finding: The operator security recommendation to set `auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT` for RFC 9068-compliant IdPs is deferred entirely to story S7, creating a documentation gap where S3 adopters have no in-band guidance that omitting this setting is a security trade-off — the reference docs generated in S3 will list the key but not surface the SHOULD-level recommendation.
findings.location: ## Security — 'S7 documents that operators SHOULD set auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT'
scenarios: An operator deploys S3 with a Cognito or Entra ID issuer that supports RFC 9068 but never enables auth_jwt_expected_typ because the docs don't call it out — leaving them exposed to cross-JWT confusion if same-issuer ID tokens share aud/iss with access tokens
findings.suggested_fix: In the `MCP_ENV_VARS` / `server.json` entry and rendered reference docs for `auth_jwt_expected_typ`, add a one-sentence note: 'For RFC 9068-compliant IdPs (e.g., Entra ID v2, Keycloak ≥ 17), set this to `at+JWT` to prevent cross-JWT confusion attacks (RFC 8725 §3.11); leave unset only if your IdP does not stamp `typ` on access tokens.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether at least one immediate retry with a short backoff is attempted on a transient JWKS fetch failure before raising, and clarify the failure mode (e.g., serve stale cache vs. hard-deny) so operators understand the blast radius of a brief IdP outage.
evidence: 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)' — explicit fail-closed, but no retry window
No mention of retry, exponential backoff, or circuit-breaker anywhere in the plan
The cooldown prevents re-fetching for up to 30 s after a failure, meaning a single failure locks out all JWKS-backed tokens for the cooldown duration
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retry or backoff on a JWKS fetch failure — 'a JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)'. A single transient network hiccup (DNS blip, IdP restart, load-balancer timeout) causes every concurrent and subsequent request to fail until the cooldown window expires and the next attempt succeeds. No circuit-breaker or degraded-mode is described.
findings.location: Security — 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises'; AC — 'Unit test asserts a JWKS fetch failure raises'
scenarios: IdP momentarily unreachable for 2 seconds; every token verification fails for the next 30-second cooldown window
Transient DNS failure causes a cascade of 401s for all authenticated endpoints

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement (and at least one AC) that the verifier emits structured log events (at WARN/ERROR level) for: JWKS fetch failure, unknown-kid cooldown activation, symmetric-algorithm startup rejection, and token verification failure with reason.
evidence: No mention of logging, metrics, structured log events, or tracing anywhere in the plan
ABSENCE: no AC requires a log line, metric counter, or trace span for any failure mode
Comparable plans (S2) typically include at least structured log events for auth failures
findings.finding: The plan introduces no observability instrumentation for the new JWKS verifier failure points: JWKS fetch failures, fetch latency, cooldown-triggered skips, token verification failures, and symmetric-algorithm startup rejections are all silent to operators. Operators cannot distinguish 'IdP is down' from 'tokens are malformed' from 'cooldown is throttling' without inspecting code.
findings.location: Security; Acceptance Criteria (all ACs); Approach & Rationale
scenarios: JWKS endpoint returns 500; all requests get 401; no log explains why — operators assume a bug in the application
Unknown-kid flood attack hits cooldown; no metric shows the flood is occurring

evidence: resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp.security') → UNRESOLVED
pyproject.toml mcp extra: 'mcp>=1.9' only — no sub-module confirmed
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as design validation, but `JWTVerifier` is unresolvable in any `mcp`/`mcp.server.fastmcp`/`mcp.server.auth` namespace in the installed environment (mcp>=1.9). If this class does not exist in the installed SDK version, the design-validation rationale is unbacked, and an implementer may be surprised to find no reference implementation to follow.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: An implementer looks for FastMCP's JWTVerifier to copy its interface and finds it does not exist in mcp>=1.9, losing the reference implementation the plan points to.
The plan's design rationale ('same shape') is cited in code review to justify interface decisions that turn out to have no grounding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether S3 or S2 adds `auth_strategies` to `McpConfig` and `_SECTIONS['mcp']` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`; if S3 owns it, add `_config_schema.py` as an explicit edit target.
evidence: src/rebar/_config_schema.py:388 — McpConfig has fields: readonly, allow_llm, allow_jira_sync only
src/rebar/_config_schema.py:538 — _SECTIONS['mcp'] coercion table: only readonly, allow_llm, allow_jira_sync
Plan says 'selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`' — implies a TOML [tool.rebar.mcp] auth_strategies key
ABSENCE: _config_schema.py is not named anywhere in the plan's edit targets or AC
findings.finding: The plan states the verifier is 'selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`', implying `auth_strategies` is a [tool.rebar.mcp] TOML config key. The current McpConfig dataclass (src/rebar/_config_schema.py:388) has only `readonly`, `allow_llm`, and `allow_jira_sync` fields, and `_SECTIONS['mcp']` contains the same three keys. Neither `_config_schema.py` nor `McpConfig` is named as an edit target by the plan. If S3 (rather than S2) is responsible for adding `auth_strategies` to the mcp config schema, this is an unenumerated edit target. If S2 adds it, the dependency covers it — but the plan does not state which story adds this key to the config schema.
findings.location: What section: 'selected by the `jwt` token in `mcp.auth_strategies`'
scenarios: Implementer adds auth_strategies reading logic in _mcp_auth.py but omits the McpConfig field and _SECTIONS['mcp'] coercion entry; config loading silently ignores the TOML key (the _warn_unknown path fires a warning but doesn't raise by default) — so the feature appears to work from env vars but TOML config is silently broken.
A follow-on story or S2 adds auth_strategies to McpConfig, but it's unclear from the plan who owns this.

The architectural claim used to justify the design pattern cannot be reviewed or validated by reviewers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `FastMCP`'s `JWTVerifier` (or its equivalent) exists in the installed `mcp` SDK (try `mcp.server.auth`, `mcp.server.fastmcp`, `fastmcp`) and either confirm the import path in the plan or replace the reference with the actual interface contract the `JWKSTokenVerifier` must satisfy.
evidence: Plan text: 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses, giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam'
resolve_symbol(name='JWTVerifier', module='mcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol(name='JWTVerifier', module='mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
Evidence class: Unverified — the specific mcp SDK surface (JWTVerifier) could not be confirmed in the installed environment
findings.finding: The Approach section cites 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses' as a design-validation prior-art reference, but JWTVerifier is UNRESOLVED in every mcp/mcp.server.fastmcp/mcp.server.auth namespace in the installed SDK. If this class does not exist in the installed mcp version, the 'same shape' rationale is unverifiable, meaning the implementer has no SDK-blessed prior-art reference to follow and must derive the verifier's interface contract from scratch.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'
scenarios: If JWTVerifier does not exist in the installed mcp SDK, the rationale cite is a false reference and the implementer must independently define the verifier's interface shape against the S2 seam — increasing design risk.
If it does exist under a different path (e.g. mcp.server.auth.JWTVerifier), the implementation might not match it, violating the 'same shape' constraint silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `typ` header is checked relative to `jwt.decode`: confirm whether it is read from `jwt.get_unverified_header()` before decode (safe because the header is covered by the signature) or extracted from decoded token headers after decode, so the implementer has a clear, unambiguous path for this security-critical check.
evidence: Plan text: 'when auth_jwt_expected_typ is set, the JWT header typ MUST equal it'
jwt.decode is verified but has no built-in required_typ kwarg in PyJWT's documented API
jwt.get_unverified_header is verified and is the standard way to read header fields pre-decode
Evidence class: Partially verified — jwt.decode exists and get_unverified_header exists, but the specific integration path for typ enforcement is unspecified
findings.finding: PyJWT's jwt.decode() does not natively accept a parameter to enforce an expected `typ` header value. Implementing the `auth_jwt_expected_typ` check requires reading the token header before or alongside decode (e.g. via jwt.get_unverified_header()) and comparing manually — but this introduces a TOCTOU window where the header read for typ-checking uses a different code path than the signature verification. The plan does not specify how the typ check is implemented relative to jwt.decode, leaving a potential implementation pitfall unaddressed.
findings.location: Security section — 'Cross-JWT-confusion typ check'; AC — 'Unit test asserts that with auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT set...'
scenarios: An implementer who reads typ via get_unverified_header() then calls jwt.decode() opens a window where a crafted token swaps the header between reads (the signature covers the original header, but the typ is read from the pre-decode path).
An implementer who checks decoded headers post-decode avoids the TOCTOU but must know that PyJWT exposes headers via the decode return value — this path is not mentioned.
findings.suggested_fix: Read `typ` from `jwt.get_unverified_header(token)` before calling `jwt.decode()`; since the header is part of the JWS-signed payload, a mismatch between pre-decode and post-decode header content will fail signature verification, making the pre-decode read safe for the typ guard.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
